schools.summary: The verse is a summary of the building history of chapters 6-8, not a date for the vision; the arithmetic of temple and palace forbids taking it chronologically.
Matthew Henry: God, who had answered by fire from heaven, now gives an express answer, appearing the night after the festival as he had at Gibeon.
Clarke: the appearance assures Solomon his service is accepted and his throne established on condition of obedience.

debates.question: Does verse 1 fix the date of the second appearance?

sides.position: No — it summarizes the whole building history; the vision came just after the dedication
Read strictly it would place the vision thirteen years later, which all reject as improbable

schools.summary: Gill: a second appearance answering Gibeon; it came the night after the dedication, not thirteen years later, as an answer to Solomon's prayer.
Keil and Delitzsch: God hallows the house by putting his name there and filling it with the cloud of his presence, giving more than Solomon asked.
Clarke: a dream, as at Gibeon, assuring acceptance of Solomon's service and a conditional establishment of his throne.
Gill: God accepted the prayer with delight; it is his prerogative to hallow the house and to fix his eye and heart on it perpetually, as on his sanctified people.
Ellicott: 'for ever' marks the temple as the settled habitation, in contrast to the tabernacle, but its larger permanence depends expressly on Israel's faithfulness.
Gill: the standard is David's uprightness in worship — not sinlessness, but a heart that never fell to idolatry.
Keil and Delitzsch: verses 4-5 answer Solomon's own petition; the throne is grounded on integrity of heart and constant obedience, in Pentateuchal language.
Matthew Henry: no religion without sincerity; the kingdom's establishment depends on the constancy of obedience.
Gill: the throne is established in Solomon's posterity until the coming of the Messiah.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: the promise repeats the Davidic covenant but is conditional; the fall of Solomon's house comments on that condition, though the line was kept 'for David's sake.'
Gill: 'if in turning ye turn' means a total, wilful apostasy to other gods, not a single lapse; the warning falls on king and nation alike.
Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: the threat is drawn from Deuteronomy and was fulfilled at the exile; Israel's survival as a 'proverb and byword' is without historical parallel.
Gill: the cutting off is the captivity of the ten tribes to Assyria and the two to Babylon; the house cast out is the temple destroyed.
Clarke: the Jews remain a proverb and byword to this day, scattered for forsaking the Lord and rejecting his Christ.
Ellicott: 'which' is absent from the Hebrew; read 'this house shall be high' (LXX) or 'for an example' (Vulg.); the height once a type of glory is here a type of ruin.
Gill: 'high' in dignity and elevation alike; the grandeur Israel gloried in makes its threatened fall the more astonishing.
Matthew Henry: the people prided themselves on the magnificent structure, but it was not too high for God's judgments.

debates.question: How should 'this house, which is high' be read?

sides.position: 'This house shall be high' (LXX) — 'which' is not in the Hebrew
'high' in esteem and elevation — the grandeur Israel gloried in

schools.summary: Ellicott: the Exodus is cited in a converse sense to Solomon's prayer — as the ground of God's claim to undivided allegiance, so their apostasy is aggravated.
Gill: the remnant left in the land will themselves confess that the ruin came because they forsook the LORD for other gods.
Clarke: 'taken hold upon other gods' is the Eastern gesture of a suppliant clasping his protector's feet, casting themselves on idols.
Keil & Delitzsch and Clarke: the twenty years cover temple (seven) and palace (thirteen); verses 10-11 form one sentence, Hiram's supply a parenthesis, the cities later restored.
Gill: the twenty years reckon from when Solomon first began to build, temple and king's house together.
Matthew Henry: the fair and friendly parting of two kings; Hiram magnified Solomon rather than envying him, and Solomon returned generosity.
Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: the grant discharged a debt for timber, gold, and stone; the cities were likely of the subject races, later restored and peopled with Israelites.
Clarke: Solomon probably gave the produce of the cities till the debt was paid, not the cities outright; they may have been conquered heathen towns.
Gill: Hiram had furnished cedar and fir for both of Solomon's houses.
Ellicott: Galilee is a 'circle' of territory near the Tyrian frontier, convenient to both kings; Hiram's discontent was with the condition of the cities, not their location.
Matthew Henry: neither king acted meanly; the land was valuable but ill-suited to a seafaring, trading people's taste.
Gill: the cities in or near Galilee lay close to Tyre, so Hiram easily came to see them.

debates.question: Why did the cities displease Hiram?

sides.position: their poor condition — they had afterward to be rebuilt
they were unsuited to the taste of a merchant, seafaring people

schools.summary: Ellicott: the derivation is uncertain; Josephus takes 'Cabul' as Phoenician for 'unpleasing'; Hiram plays on the name of a border town to signal his discontent.
Clarke: the learned disagree; some read 'Cabul' as 'border' (so the LXX), from the Asherite town of that name.
Gill: Hiram's complaint was conveyed by letter.

sides.position: a Phoenician word for 'displeasing,' used by Hiram as a play on words

schools.summary: Ellicott: a huge sum, a sixth of Solomon's yearly revenue; whether it explains verse 11's gold or acknowledges the cities is conjecture, but it leads into the account of the levy.
Gill: render 'had sent' — the gold Hiram furnished for the temple, an immense sum by the old reckonings.
Clarke: the sum Hiram had lent, to repay which Solomon taxed his people, as the next verses show.

debates.question: Which way did the sixscore talents flow?

sides.position: gold Hiram had already sent Solomon (or paid for the cities)
a loan Hiram made, repaid by Solomon's levy on the people

schools.summary: Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: the levy funded fortress-cities of key strategic value — Hazor, Megiddo, Gezer, and the Millo citadel — guarding the land's invasion routes.
Clarke: the levy repaid Hiram's loan and was a grievous, oppressive tax, the grievance later voiced to Rehoboam.
Matthew Henry: Solomon built God's house first and prospered after — 'if we begin with God, he will go on with us.'
Gill: the levy was of men to work and money to defray the expense.
Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: Pharaoh took and burnt Gezer, slew its Canaanites, and gave it as a dowry to his daughter; the motive of the campaign is unknown.
Clarke: Gezer, taken by Joshua but re-held by Canaanites, was retaken by Pharaoh and given to Solomon in dowry.
Gill: Pharaoh 'went up' to the higher land of Canaan and burnt Gezer, his motive uncertain.

Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: 'built' means fortified; Gezer and the two Beth-horons guarded the passes from the maritime plain into the hill country — Beth-horon famed for three of Israel's victories.
Gill: Solomon rebuilt Gezer, largely burnt by Pharaoh.
Keil & Delitzsch: Baalath is the Danite city near Gezer, not Baalbek; Tadmor (the Chronicler's reading, the Arabs' name still) is Palmyra, on the caravan road to the Euphrates.
Clarke: Tadmor is Palmyra, whose ruins give the highest idea of Solomon's splendour, later home of Zenobia and Longinus.
Gill: Baalath was a city in the tribe of Dan.
Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: store-cities held provisions for army and people; chariot-cities projected power; 'that which he desired' points to the pleasure-gardens of Ecclesiastes and the Song.
Clarke: the splendour added to the reign, but the subjects were heavily oppressed by the taxation it required.
Gill: the cities of store held magazines of corn, arms, and ammunition.

Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: the Canaanite remnant bore the forced labour while Israelites served as soldiers and officers; the institution predated Solomon, from the Conquest, and he merely organised it.
Gill: the labourers were the Canaanite peoples left from Joshua's day, not native Israelites, though some were proselytes.
Matthew Henry: in enslaving the heathen rather than Israel, Solomon kept the Law and fulfilled Noah's curse on Canaan.
Gill: the labourers were the posterity of the Canaanites left unsubdued in Joshua's day, on whom Solomon levied bond-service.
Clarke: they did the most laborious public works while Israel was exempt, like the captives Sesostris used to build Egypt's temples.
Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: Israel served as warriors and officers, not slaves; yet the exemption, kept in theory, was strained in Solomon's later despotism, as the grievance to Rehoboam shows.
Gill: enslaving Israelites was against the Law — they might be hired servants but not bondmen.
Matthew Henry: Solomon preserved the dignity and liberty of Israel, God's freemen, a kingdom of priests.
Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: the 550 are the standing corps of overseers; the larger figure in 1 Kings 5:16 refers to the special temple levy, so the numbers do not conflict.
Gill: the chief officers set over the building of the houses and cities.
Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: the Chronicler gives the reason — the places are holy where the ark had come; the move is tied to the building of the Millo, and she 'came up' to Zion's higher summit.
Gill: Solomon would not let his Egyptian wife dwell in the house of David, hallowed by the ark's former presence.

Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: the three feasts are Passover, Weeks, and Tabernacles; the temple ended the high-place sacrifices, and Solomon did not usurp the priest's office but renewed the royal offering.
Gill: the three times were Passover, Pentecost, and Tabernacles, but not his only sacrifices; the priests offered at the brazen altar.
Clarke: the three times were Passover, Pentecost, and Tabernacles.
Matthew Henry: Solomon built the altar and then used it himself — many help others' devotions yet neglect their own.

Keil & Delitzsch and Ellicott: Ezion-geber near Eloth sits at the head of the Gulf of Akabah in Edom; the barren country supplied no timber, so ships and materials came from Tyre with Phoenician crews.
Gill: the port was a wilderness station whose name, 'the backbone of a man,' came from a ridge of rocks before it.
Clarke: the word is literally singular — perhaps only one ship, manned chiefly by Tyrians.

Ellicott and Keil & Delitzsch: the Tyrians were the great seamen and Israel had little care for the sea; the joint fleet paired Tyrian skill with Israelite gold, an enterprise exceptional for a separate people.
Gill: he weighs how Hiram's men reached the Red Sea from Mediterranean Tyre, noting this is the first navy recorded and the Tyrians its masters.
Ellicott: only this is certain — Ophir lay east of Palestine, reached by the Red Sea, its gold proverbial; the evidence leans toward India, the rest speculation.
Gill: theories range from Africa to America; he judges Malacca, the 'golden Chersonese,' most reasonable, with Josephus.
Clarke: no one knows for certain; the three-year voyage suits the monsoons, and he inclines to Sofala in Africa.

sides.position: India or the far East (the 'golden Chersonese')
Africa (Sofala), reached on a three-year monsoon voyage
